turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: Now, the second case is called Midland Funding versus Johnson. Midland Funding filed a claim as a creditor in a bankruptcy, a personal bankruptcy of Aleida Johnson. The proof of claim that it filed said that Johnson owed Midland Funding a credit card debt of about $1,900. It was a problem for Midland. That collection effort it was making was on a credit card where the last charge had made ten years earlier, and the statute of limitations is just six years. Johnson's lawyer objected that Midland could not get that money. And the bankruptcy court said, “You are absolutely right. It is out of date.” They disallowed it. Then, later, Johnson brought a lawsuit against Midland seeking damages, special damages, under the Fair Debt Collection Practices Act. Now, that act, at least as far as it is relevant here, says a debt collector, and we count Midland as a debt collector, cannot make a “False, deceptive, or misleading representation or engage in any unfair or unconscionable means to collect a debt. Do any of those terms apply to what Midland did? The district court thought none of them applied. All It did was assert an untimely claim. That might not be such a great thing, but it does not fall within those terms. The Eleventh Circuit, however, reversed. On the contrary.' We are asked to decide who is right. And we end up agreeing with the District Court. We think that the act, the Fair Debt Collection Act, does not apply those five terms. We think it is fairly clear that Midland's proof of claim, though it was filed after the stature of limitations period had run, was not false, deceptive, or misleading. Ordinarily in the law, untimeliness is an affirmative defense to be raised by the plaintiff -- by the defendant, rather. The plaintiff, which is in effect, Midland, does not have to assert it. He can assert the claim and then the defendant has to raise it. And so, he did not do anything misleading or deceptive. We show all that by going through the Bankruptcy Code, the words there fairly technical. Now, whether filing an untimely claim is unfair or unconscionable is a much closer question. But ultimately, we conclude it was not. Johnson points out that several lower courts have indicated in the context of an ordinary civil action to collect a debt that a debt collector's assertion of the time of the claim known to be time-barred is unfair. But the lower courts rested their conclusions primarily on their concern that a consumer might unwittingly repay a time-barred debt. And here, in this bankruptcy context, that concern has less force. A personal bankruptcy involves a trustee who has to examine the proofs of claim and who will, as here, pose objections where appropriate, including objections based on untimeliness which when they are valid will lead to disallowance of the claim. That is what happened. Moreover, the debtor initiates the bankruptcy proceeding. Procedural bankruptcy rules are more -- more directly guide the evaluation of claims. And the claims resolution process is more streamlined. All that is to say that the consumer or the debtor is less likely to be taken unawares when it is a personal bankruptcy through the filing of an untimely claim. At the same time, bankruptcy proceedings are special in that the Bankruptcy Code, as this Court has said, reflects a delicate balance of a debtor's protections and obligations. Perhaps for that reas'on, the Advisory Committee on the Bankruptcy Rules considered that it rejected a proposal that would have changed the nature of the affirmative untimeliness defense. So they said, “Do not do it.” We do not believe In a different context, We do not believe that Congress, in the Fair Debt Collection Practices Act, intended to change that Bankruptcy Code balance. For these and other reasons set forth in our opinion, we conclude that a creditor's filing of an untimely claim is not just by itself a false, deceptive, misleading, unfair, or unconscionable debt collection practice, as those terms are used in the Fair Debt Collection Practices Act. So, we reverse the contrary determination of the Eleventh Circuit. Justice Sotomayor has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justices Ginsberg and Kagan have joined. Justice Gorsuch took no part in the consideration or decision of this case.
